content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Robert C. Summers
     Franchise Tax Board

Date : August 3, 1976

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Kenneth Finney

Subject: Regulations §§ 18215 and 18225

I am in receipt of your memorandum of July 2, 1976, seeking
clarification with respect to the operation of regulations §§ 18215
and 18225. The response to the various questions, which correspond
to the numbering in your memorandum, follow:

1.a.(1)    You ask whether, pursuant to subsection (a) of Section
           18215, action designed to influence or to attempt to
           influence the voters against the passage or qualification of a measure
           would be within the definition of political purposes. The answer is
           yes. If, however, you believe that the present punctuation creates
           a problem, we can discuss this matter further.

1.a.(2)    You ask whether subsection (a) of Section 18215 applies
           to the body of the regulation as well as to Gov. Code
           Section 82015. Again, the answer is yes. However, with respect to those
           persons or entities specified in Section 18215(b), payments received are,
           by definition, automatically for political purposes.

1.b.       You inquire whether the definition of "made at the behest
           of," which includes payments made "under the control or
           at the direction of," includes situations where the beneficiary has
           knowledge of the payment made on his behalf. You then illustrate the
           question with an example which involves an individual paying a liability
           on behalf of a committee. Your answer to the general question is that
           more knowledge does not necessarily equal "under the control or at the
           direction of." In other words, if a beneficiary is merely aware that
           someone is doing something on his behalf, an in-kind contribution will
           not necessarily result. However, in the context of an individual
           discharging a liability for someone else, it is unlikely that this
           particular form of activity would not be done under the control or at
           the direction of the beneficiary. This is because the beneficiary
           necessarily would have to provide the individual who is discharging
           the liability with information about the liability and thus the requisite
           control or direction undoubtedly would exist.

1.c.(1)    You ask whether an intermediary or transfer agent who only
           withholds and distributes earmarked funds qualifies as a
           person subject to the provisions of subsection (d) of Section 18215.
           The mere fact that someone acts as an intermediary or transfer agent,
           standing alone, does not mean that they will become a committee by
           reason of the receipt and subsequent distribution of earmarked funds.

B

82025

76-08-075

Robert Summons
August 3, 1976
Page Two

The donors of the funds and the ultimate recipients of the funds are
the persons who must document the other criteria established
by the Political Reform Act are satisfied.

1.c.(2)     You ask what the definition is of full and adequate considera-
tion. There is no operative definition of full and adequate
consideration which is applicable to every case. Instead, the staff
addresses the question on a case by case basis. However, the standard
entails something which is higher than the contract standard of good
consideration but lower than the standard of precisely equal consideration.

1.c.(3)     You ask what the definition is of a reasonable basis for
apportionment. The staff has adopted the view that the
best policy with respect to apportionment is to permit the individual
who must do the apportioning maximum flexibility, provided that the
individual uses good faith in an attempt to accurately reflect the
activity he is reporting. In other words, again there is not one
operable definition and it will depend upon the facts of the particular
case. Of course, specific earmarking of a portion of a payment by the
donor or the recipient will negate the need for apportioning.

2.a.     Your questions are the same as questions 1.a.(1) and (2),
and the answers are the same.

2.b.(1)     You ask whether subsection (c) of Section 18225 includes
indirect communication expenditures. By way of example,
you ask: When a person contributes $1,000 to committee and the
committee in turn expends the $1,000 for a communication which expressly
advocates the election of a candidate that it is supporting, does the
individual who contributed the $1,000 have to report the expenditure.
The individual who contributes the $1,000 would report the payment,
if at all, as a $1,000 contribution made. 1/     The individual would not
be required to report the $1,000 as an expenditure.

2.b.(2)     You ask whether expenditures of the type described in sub-
section (c) of Section 18225 of less than $500 are reportable.
Such expenditures are not reportable unless they are incurred by an
entity which already is a committee. In other words, the same criteri-
which specifies when an entity becomes a committee are applicable in the
case of expenditures described in subsection (c).

2.c.     You ask whether a nonmonetary payment, as described in
Section 18225, is synonymous with the term payment defined
in Gov. Code Section 82044. The answer is yes. The definition of pay-
ment in Section 82044 is the operative definition for the term payment
as used in Section 18225.

In addition to the questions posed in your memorandum, Norm
Fuller called to inquire about the following hypothetical concerning
the definition of contribution (Section 82015): A purchases a ticket
for a fundraising dinner for $25.00 and receives a meal worth $10.00.

_________________
1/ I say if at all because the individual would have no
reporting obligation unless he otherwise qualified as a committee under
Section 82013(b) or (c).

Robert Summer
August 3, 1976
Page Three


Is the amount of A's contribution $15.00 or $25.00? The amount
of the contribution is $25.00. In the context of underwriting
dinners, if full and adequate consideration is not received,
the full amount of the payment is the contribution, not only
the difference. I want to caution you, however, that this is
not necessarily a rule of general applicability. For example,
in the context of a rummage sale, the staff has been advising
people that when the purchase price for the rummage substantially
exceeds its fair market values, only the difference between the
purchase price and the fair market value of the rummage is the
amount of the contribution.

In conclusion, many of your questions are abstract
and my responses, therefore, are rather general. If you want
to discuss these matters in more detail, please do not hesitate
to contact me.




KF:pvb

State of California                                                    Franchise Tax Board

Memorandum

To : Mickey Bennett              JUL 5 1976 10        Date : July 2, 1976
     FPPC Executive Director
                                                       File No.: ROS:cf


From : R. C. Summers

Subject: Regulations 18215 and 18225


     Discussions with our staff regarding the subject regulations have
uncovered some items that need further clarification.  They are:

1.  Regulation 18215 - Contributions

    a.  Subsection (a) - Political purposes definition

        (1)  Is the structure of the paragraph and punctuation
             utilized appropriate to indicate that the influencing
             or attempting to influence the action of the voters
             against the passage or qualifications of a measure
             is within the definition of political purposes?

             It would be clearer if structured as follows:  Political
             purposes as used in Government Code Section 82015, means
             made for the purpose of influencing or attempting to
             influence the action of the voters for or against:  the
             nomination or election of a candidate or candidates;
             or qualifications or passage of any measure.

        (2)  Does subsection (a) apply equally to the body of
             regulation 18215 or only to the language contained in
             Section 82015 of the Government Code?

    b.  Subsection (c) - Definition of "made at the behest of" -
        Does "under the control or at the direction of" include
        knowledge of?  For example, Committee X incurs a liability
        with Mr. Y and Mr. Z, informs Committee X that he will pay
        Mr. Y.  Is this payment made under the control or at the
        direction of Committee X, as defined by subsection (c)?
        Would the answer be different if Mr. Z had not informed
        Committee X?

    c.  Subsection (d) - Earmarked contributions

        (1)  Does an intermediary or transfer agent who only withholds
             and distributes earmarked funds qualify as a person or
             organization subject to the provisions of subsection (d)?
             An example might be the State of California acting as a
             transfer agent of earmarked funds to the CSEA.

Mickey Bennet
July 2, 1976
Page 2


     (2) What is the definition of full and adequate consideration?
         As an example does a member of OSEA receive full and
         adequate consideration for his "earmarked" contribution?

     (3) What is the definition of a reasonable basis for
         apportionment? Does it include specific identification?
         What factors are relevant in determining an allocation
         basis?

2. Regulation 18225 - Expenditures

   a. Subsection (a) - Political purposes definition - same questions
      referred to in 1. a. (1) & (2).

   b. Subsection (c) - Communication expenditures

      (1) Does the meaning include indirect communication
          expenditures? For example, Mr. X contributes $1,000 to
          Y Committee. Y Committee uses the $1,000 for communication
          expenditures. Since Mr. X's payment was used for
          communications, is he required to report the transaction?

      (2) If expenditures for communications under subsection (c)are
          less than $500, is the person or organization required
          to report such? If yes, under what requirement and using
          what forms?

   c. Subsection (b)-(c) - Payment

      Section 82044 defining payment includes the rendering of
      property, services or anything else of value. Is nonmonetary
      payment as used in regulation 18225 restricted to the definition
      referred to or is it intended to be more all inclusive?

Your prompt reply will be appreciated.


Chief, Political Reform Audit Division

cc: N. Fuller
    J. Pavalasky
    K. Waldrum
    File
    M. Huff

sections.analysis:
s with our staff regarding the subject regulations have uncovered some items that need further clarification. They are:

1. Regulation 18215 - Contributions

   a. Subsection (a) - Political purposes definition

      (1) Is the structure of the paragraph and punctuation utilized appropriate to indicate that the influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters against the passage or qualifications of a measure is within the definition of political purposes?

      It would be clearer if structured as follows: Political purposes as used in Government Code Section 82015, means made for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against: the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates; or qualifications or passage of any measure.

      (2) Does subsection (a) apply equally to the body of regulation 18215 or only to the language contained in Section 82015 of the Government Code?

   b. Subsection (c) - Definition of "made at the behest of" - Does "under the control or at the direction of" include knowledge of? For example, Committee X incurs a liability with Mr. Y and Mr. Z, informs Committee X that he will pay Mr. Y. Is this payment made under the control or at the direction of Committee X, as defined by subsection (c)? Would the answer be different if Mr. Z had not informed Committee X?

   c. Subsection (d) - Earmarked contributions

      (1) Does an intermediary or transfer agent who only withholds and distributes earmarked funds qualify as a person or organization subject to the provisions of subsection (d)? An example might be the State of California acting as a transfer agent of earmarked funds to the CSEA.

(2) What is the definition of full and adequate consideration? As an example does a member of CSEA receive full and adequate consideration for his "earmarked" contribution?

(3) What is the definition of a reasonable basis for apportionment? Does it include specific identification? What factors are relevant in determining an allocation basis?

2. Regulation 18225 - Expenditures

a. Subsection (a) - Political purposes definition - same questions referred to in l. a. (1) & (2).

b. Subsection (c) - Communication expenditures

(1) Does the meaning include indirect communication expenditures? For example, Mr. X contributes $1,000 to Y Committee. Y Committee uses the $1,000 for communication expenditures. Since Mr. X's payment was used for communications, is he required to report the transaction?

(2) If expenditures for communications under subsection (c) are less than $500, is the person or organization required to report such? If yes, under what requirement and using what forms?

c. Subsection (b)-(c) - Payment

Section 82044 defining payment includes the rendering of property, services or anything else of value. Is nonmonetary payment as used in regulation 18225 restricted to the definition referred to or is it intended to be more all inclusive?

Your prompt reply will be appreciated.

Chief, Political Reform Audit Division

cc: N. Fuller
    J. Pavalasky
    K. Waldrum
    File
    M. Huff
